steps.annotation: Create a labeled box named balance and put 100 inside.
Read the box (100), add 50, write 150 back into the same box.
Print whatever the box currently holds: 150.

levelLabel: L2 -> reading vs writing the box

steps.annotation: Box balance starts holding 200.
Box deposit starts holding 75.
Read both boxes, sum is 275, write 275 into balance.

teachMe: The box balance first holds 100, then is overwritten with 250. cout reads the latest value.
balance + 25 reads the box (50), computes 75, and writes 75 back into balance.
Each subtraction overwrites balance with the new total.
savings = balance copies the value out of balance into savings; balance is unchanged.

clozeSentence: Fill in the variable name to overwrite the box.
explanation: We are writing into the existing box named balance.

clozeSentence: Fill in the operator to add 50 to the box.
explanation: Reading balance, adding 50, then writing the sum back uses the + operator.

clozeSentence: Fill in the stream operator that prints the box.
explanation: cout uses << to send the value to the terminal.

clozeSentence: After balance is overwritten with 0, what does cout print?
explanation: The latest write is balance = 0, so cout prints 0.

spec: Write a single line that creates an int box named balance holding the value 100.
explanation: type, name, =, value, semicolon — that is the full declaration with initialization.

spec: Write the line that overwrites an existing int variable balance with 0.
explanation: Assignment uses just name = value;. The type was declared earlier so don't repeat int.

spec: Write two lines: declare an int balance starting at 100, then add 50 to it.
explanation: First declare and init, then read-modify-write to update the box.

A different name, target, refers to a different box.

Use snake_case names that say what the box holds.
Each variable has its own unique name.
Reading by name returns 80 from the weight_kg box.

weight and weight2 are completely separate boxes; the trailing 2 makes a new name.
Writing to kg does not affect lb; they are independent named boxes.
copy got 70 from weight, but later changing weight does not change copy.

levelLabel: L2 -> type decides what fits in the box

steps.annotation: int box: only whole numbers fit.
double box: decimals allowed.
string box: text in double quotes.
int discards the .99 you might have wanted.
double keeps the decimal part.
Prints 9 — the int box has no fraction.
Prints 9.99 — the double held the full value.

teachMe: int holds 9 only; double can hold 9.99 with the decimal.
Storing a double in an int truncates the decimal part. 9.99 becomes 9.
Mixing int (3) and double (1.5) in math gives a double result (4.5).
Two different types print fine to cout; each box keeps its own type.

clozeSentence: Fill in the type that holds a decimal number.
explanation: 9.99 has a decimal — the type must be double (or float).

clozeSentence: Fill in the type that holds a whole number.
explanation: 3 is a whole number — int is the standard integer type.

clozeSentence: Fill in the type that holds text.
explanation: Text in double quotes is stored in a string.

clozeSentence: What value ends up in the int copy?
explanation: Storing 9.99 into an int truncates the decimal — copy holds 9.

levelLabel: L2 -> reading a variable to print

steps.annotation: Set up the box.
Using the name reads the box; cout prints 1.75.
height holds 1.75.
Read height (1.75), divide by 2, store 0.875 in half.
Print half: 0.875.

teachMe: cout << height; reads the box without changing it, and prints the value.
cout reads height (1.8) and the literal " m" and prints them in order.
Reading does not change the box. Printing it twice prints the same value twice.
= reads the right side (height) and writes the value into copy.

clozeSentence: Fill in the variable being read on the right side.
explanation: = reads the right side; height's value (1.80) flows into copy.

clozeSentence: Fill in the punctuation that ends the cout statement.

quantity holds 10.

Each line reads the latest value of quantity and writes a new one.
quantity = price copies price's value into quantity. price is unchanged.

explanation: The latest assignment is 25, so cout prints 25.

steps.annotation: Single = assigns: status now holds true.
Double == compares: yields a bool, no write.
Compare was true, so the if body runs.
= writes 0 into status.
== compares; status holds 0, not 1.
Comparison was false, so we enter else.
Prints idle.

teachMe: Single = assigns. Two assignments later, status holds 1.
== compares; 5 == 5 is true so the if body runs and prints match.
After status = 1, status == 0 is false, so the else branch runs.
== returns a bool. cout prints true as 1 by default.

clozeSentence: Fill in the operator that assigns.
explanation: Single = is assignment.

clozeSentence: Fill in the operator that compares status to 1.
explanation: Double == is the equality comparison operator.

clozeSentence: Fill in the operator that compares status to 0 producing a bool.
explanation: == returns a bool that goes into same.

clozeSentence: Fill in the literal that prints match (with quotes).
explanation: String literals need double quotes.

steps.annotation: Declaration must come first.

Declare first — compiler now knows the name.
Use after declaration — compiler accepts it.

teachMe: Declaration on line 1 makes the name temperature usable on line 2.

Line 2 uses temperature, which was declared on line 1 — that order is required.
Each later line uses the latest value of temperature.

int math is exact for whole numbers as long as no division truncation happens.

levelLabel: L2 -> bool holds true or false

steps.annotation: bool box holds true.
if reads passed; true means body runs.
Prints A.
score holds 92.
>= produces a bool. 92 >= 50 → true.
cout prints bool true as 1 by default.

teachMe: bool true prints as 1 by default; bool false would print as 0.
(score >= 50) yields the bool true; cout prints true as 1.
if (false) skips the body; the else branch runs.

prompt: Declare char user_id set to the char literal 'A'.

question: Type the canonical char declaration: char user_id = 'A';

levelLabel: L2 -> true and false are bool literals

steps.annotation: true is one of two bool literals.
false is the other bool literal.

fullCode:
bool a = true;
bool b = false;

steps.annotation: Always lowercase: true (not True or TRUE).
Always lowercase: false.

teachMe: true literal stored in bool prints as 1.
false literal stored in bool prints as 0.
Multiple reassignments; only the last value (true → 1) is printed.
if (true) runs; if (false) is skipped. Only "a" prints.

code:
bool capacity_ok = ___;
// literal that means yes
clozeSentence: Fill in the bool literal that means yes.
explanation: true is the bool literal for the yes/affirmative case.

code:
bool capacity_ok = ___;
// literal that means no
clozeSentence: Fill in the bool literal that means no.
explanation: false is the bool literal for the no/negative case.

clozeSentence: Fill in the type that holds true or false.
explanation: bool holds the two bool literals true and false.

clozeSentence: Fill in the bool literal to flip capacity_ok.
explanation: false is the opposite of true; reassignment overwrites the box.

spec: Declare a bool variable capacity_ok set to true.
explanation: bool + the literal true.

spec: Declare a bool variable capacity_ok set to false.
explanation: bool + the literal false.

spec: Declare bool a = true; and bool b = false; on separate lines.
expectedAnswer:
bool a = true;
bool b = false;
explanation: Two bool declarations using each of the two bool literals.

prompt: Declare bool a = true and bool b = false on two lines.
expectedAnswer:
bool a = true;
bool b = false;

variants.prompt: Declare bool ok = true and bool fail = false.
variants.expectedAnswer:
bool ok = true;
bool fail = false;

variants.prompt: Declare bool found = true and bool empty = false.
variants.expectedAnswer:
bool found = true;
bool empty = false;

prompt: Declare bool capacity_ok set to true.

question: Type a bool declaration using literal true: bool capacity_ok = true;

steps.annotation: Brings in cin/cout.
Required to use the string type.
Without #include <string>, this line would not compile.
Needed for cin and cout.
Needed whenever you use the string type.
Lets you write string instead of std::string.

teachMe: Both #includes are present, so the string and cout work; ratio prints 3:1.
All three string operations rely on #include <string> being at the top.
string default-constructs to "". Then assignment fills it. Both rely on <string>.
string + operator also requires #include <string>.

clozeSentence: Fill in the preprocessor directive that loads a header.
explanation: #include is the directive; <string> is the header.

clozeSentence: Fill in the header for the string type.
explanation: <string> provides the string type.

clozeSentence: Fill in the header name (between < and >).
explanation: <string> = the C++ standard string header.

clozeSentence: Fill in the namespace name.
explanation: std is the C++ standard library namespace.

spec: Write the single line that includes the string header.
explanation: #include + the header in angle brackets.

spec: Write the three top lines: include iostream, include string, using namespace std.
explanation: These three lines start any program that uses cout/cin AND string.

spec: Write a complete program that includes <iostream> and <string>, declares string ratio = "3:1";, and prints it from main, returning 0.
explanation: Full minimal program: both includes, namespace, main, declare-and-print, return 0.

examples.label: Example A: only iostream
Example B: iostream + string

prompt: Write a complete minimal program that uses both cout and a string variable greeting = "hi".

question: Type the three header lines in order: iostream, string, using namespace std.
